Description: A trait should represent one capability or behavior. Small traits enable flexible composition and easier implementation
Do: Define traits with 1-5 methods; split large traits into smaller composable ones; use supertraits for requirements
Dont: Create God traits with many unrelated methods; force implementors to implement methods they do not need

Guideline: Prefer impl Trait over dyn Trait
Keywords: impl Trait, static dispatch, monomorphization, generics
Description: Use impl Trait or generic bounds for static dispatch which enables inlining and avoids vtable overhead
Do: Use impl Trait in argument position and return position for zero-cost abstraction; use generics when the caller needs to name the type
Dont: Use dyn Trait everywhere when static dispatch would work; use trait objects in hot paths without benchmarking

Guideline: Use Trait Objects for Heterogeneous Collections
Keywords: dyn Trait, Box, Vec, heterogeneous, runtime
Description: When a collection must hold different types implementing the same trait, use Box<dyn Trait>
Do: Use Box<dyn Trait> for heterogeneous collections; ensure trait is object-safe; use Arc<dyn Trait> for shared ownership across threads
Dont: Avoid trait objects entirely when heterogeneous storage is needed; use enum when the set of types is truly open

Keywords: object safe, dyn, Sized, Self, generic
Description: Understand what makes a trait object-safe: no Self in return types, no generic methods, no associated types with constraints
Do: Avoid using Self as return type; avoid generic methods; use associated types instead of generic type parameters when possible
Dont: Accidentally make traits non-object-safe then wonder why Box<dyn Trait> fails to compile

Description: Provide default method implementations to reduce boilerplate for implementors
Do: Provide sensible defaults that can be overridden; build complex behavior from simpler required methods
Dont: Require implementors to duplicate logic that could have a default; make all methods default when some should be required

Description: Use supertraits to require that a type implementing a trait also implements other traits
Do: Use supertraits for real dependencies (Debug + Display); keep the chain short; document why supertrait is required
Dont: Create deep inheritance chains; use supertraits when a bound on specific methods would suffice

Keywords: associated type, generic, type parameter, Iterator
Description: Use associated types for traits where each implementation has exactly one type; use generics when multiple implementations per type are valid
Do: Use associated types for Iterator::Item, Deref::Target; use generics for From<T>, Add<Rhs>
Dont: Use generic type parameters when there is only one valid implementation per type; overcomplicate APIs with unnecessary generics

Keywords: blanket, impl, for all, generic impl
Description: Implement a trait for all types that satisfy certain bounds to provide automatic functionality
Do: Use blanket impls for universal adapters (impl<T: Display> ToString for T); check for conflicts with specific impls
Dont: Create conflicting blanket impls; use blanket impls when only specific types should implement the trait

Description: Prevent external crates from implementing a trait by requiring a private supertrait
Do: Use a sealed trait when you need to add methods later without breaking changes; mark with a private Sealed supertrait
Dont: Make all public traits unsealed when you need to control the implementation set

Description: Add methods to types you do not own by defining a trait and implementing it for the external type
Do: Use extension traits to add domain methods to std/external types; use a clear naming convention like TypeExt
Dont: Use extension traits when a free function is clearer; create extension traits with too many methods

Description: Accept AsRef<T> or AsMut<T> in function signatures for maximum flexibility with reference types
Do: Use impl AsRef<Path> for path arguments; use impl AsRef<str> for string-like arguments; use impl AsRef<[u8]> for byte-like arguments
Dont: Require specific types like &str or &Path when AsRef would make the function more flexible

Description: Display is for end-user-facing output; Debug is for developer diagnostics. Implement both when appropriate
Do: Derive Debug for all types; implement Display for types shown to users; use # for pretty Debug formatting
Dont: Implement only Debug and use it for user-facing output; implement Display that shows internal fields

Description: Implement Iterator for custom types to enable for loops and iterator combinators
Do: Implement Iterator with type Item and fn next(); implement IntoIterator for owned and borrowed iteration
Dont: Return a Vec and force callers to iterate over it; implement Iterator on the collection itself instead of a separate iterator type

Description: Implement Deref for types that wrap a single inner value and should transparently expose its methods
Do: Implement Deref for smart pointers and newtype wrappers; example: String derefs to str, Vec<T> derefs to [T]
Dont: Implement Deref for types that are not logically a smart pointer or wrapper; abuse Deref for inheritance-like behavior

Description: Use where clauses for complex trait bounds to keep function signatures clean
Do: Use where clauses when bounds are complex or reference multiple type parameters; keep simple single bounds inline
Dont: Cram complex bounds into the generic parameter list making the signature unreadable

Keywords: Add, Sub, Mul, ops, operator, overload
Description: Implement operator traits from std::ops to enable natural syntax for domain types
Do: Implement operators when the semantics are clear and expected; implement both owned and reference versions
Dont: Overload operators with surprising semantics; implement Add for types where addition is not intuitive

Description: Use proc macro derives to automatically implement traits for custom types
Do: Use derive macros for repetitive trait implementations; use popular crates like serde and thiserror; create custom derives for domain traits
Dont: Manually implement traits that could be derived; create derive macros for non-repetitive or complex logic

Keywords: Send, Sync, thread safety, marker, auto
Description: Send means a type can be transferred across threads. Sync means a type can be shared via references across threads
Do: Design types to be Send + Sync when possible; use Arc for shared ownership; understand when these auto traits are not implemented
Dont: Manually implement Send/Sync with unsafe without understanding the implications; use Rc<RefCell<T>> when Arc<Mutex<T>> is needed

Description: Implement methods or traits conditionally based on trait bounds of generic parameters
Do: Add trait implementations or methods that are only available when generic parameters satisfy specific bounds
Dont: Require all trait bounds upfront even when only some methods need them

Keywords: trait alias, bound, shorthand, type alias
Description: Create type aliases for complex trait bound combinations to reduce repetition
Do: Use trait aliases for bounds used in multiple places; document what the combined bound means
Dont: Repeat complex bounds across many function signatures
